title: 第 1 章
original: धृतराष्ट्र उवाच धर्मक्षेत्रे कुरुक्षेत्रे समवेता युयुत्सवः। मामकाः पाण्डवाश्चैव किमकुर्वत सञ्जय।।1.1।।
सञ्जय उवाच दृष्ट्वा तु पाण्डवानीकं व्यूढं दुर्योधनस्तदा। आचार्यमुपसङ्गम्य राजा वचनमब्रवीत्।।1.2।।
पश्यैतां पाण्डुपुत्राणामाचार्य महतीं चमूम्। व्यूढां द्रुपदपुत्रेण तव शिष्येण धीमता।।1.3।।
अत्र शूरा महेष्वासा भीमार्जुनसमा युधि। युयुधानो विराटश्च द्रुपदश्च महारथः।।1.4।।
धृष्टकेतुश्चेकितानः काशिराजश्च वीर्यवान्। पुरुजित्कुन्तिभोजश्च शैब्यश्च नरपुङ्गवः।।1.5।।
युधामन्युश्च विक्रान्त उत्तमौजाश्च वीर्यवान्। सौभद्रो द्रौपदेयाश्च सर्व एव महारथाः।।1.6।।
अस्माकं तु विशिष्टा ये तान्निबोध द्विजोत्तम। नायका मम सैन्यस्य संज्ञार्थं तान्ब्रवीमि ते।।1.7।।
भवान्भीष्मश्च कर्णश्च कृपश्च समितिञ्जयः। अश्वत्थामा विकर्णश्च सौमदत्तिस्तथैव च।।1.8।।
अन्ये च बहवः शूरा मदर्थे त्यक्तजीविताः। नानाशस्त्रप्रहरणाः सर्वे युद्धविशारदाः।।1.9।।
अपर्याप्तं तदस्माकं बलं भीष्माभिरक्षितम्। पर्याप्तं त्विदमेतेषां बलं भीमाभिरक्षितम्।।1.10।।
अयनेषु च सर्वेषु यथाभागमवस्थिताः। भीष्ममेवाभिरक्षन्तु भवन्तः सर्व एव हि।।1.11।।
तस्य संजनयन्हर्षं कुरुवृद्धः पितामहः। सिंहनादं विनद्योच्चैः शङ्खं दध्मौ प्रतापवान्।।1.12।।
ततः शङ्खाश्च भेर्यश्च पणवानकगोमुखाः। सहसैवाभ्यहन्यन्त स शब्दस्तुमुलोऽभवत्।।1.13।।
ततः श्वेतैर्हयैर्युक्ते महति स्यन्दने स्थितौ। माधवः पाण्डवश्चैव दिव्यौ शङ्खौ प्रदध्मतुः।।1.14।।
पाञ्चजन्यं हृषीकेशो देवदत्तं धनंजयः। पौण्ड्रं दध्मौ महाशङ्खं भीमकर्मा वृकोदरः।।1.15।।
अनन्तविजयं राजा कुन्तीपुत्रो युधिष्ठिरः। नकुलः सहदेवश्च सुघोषमणिपुष्पकौ।।1.16।।
काश्यश्च परमेष्वासः शिखण्डी च महारथः। धृष्टद्युम्नो विराटश्च सात्यकिश्चापराजितः।।1.17।।
द्रुपदो द्रौपदेयाश्च सर्वशः पृथिवीपते। सौभद्रश्च महाबाहुः शङ्खान्दध्मुः पृथक्पृथक्।।1.18।।
स घोषो धार्तराष्ट्राणां हृदयानि व्यदारयत्। नभश्च पृथिवीं चैव तुमुलो व्यनुनादयन्।।1.19।।
अथ व्यवस्थितान् दृष्ट्वा धार्तराष्ट्रान्कपिध्वजः। प्रवृत्ते शस्त्रसंपाते धनुरुद्यम्य पाण्डवः।।1.20।।
अर्जुन उवाच हृषीकेशं तदा वाक्यमिदमाह महीपते। सेनयोरुभयोर्मध्ये रथं स्थापय मेऽच्युत।।1.21।।
यावदेतान्निरीक्षेऽहं योद्धुकामानवस्थितान्। कैर्मया सह योद्धव्यमस्मिन्रणसमुद्यमे।।1.22।।
योत्स्यमानानवेक्षेऽहं य एतेऽत्र समागताः। धार्तराष्ट्रस्य दुर्बुद्धेर्युद्धे प्रियचिकीर्षवः।।1.23।।
संजय उवाच एवमुक्तो हृषीकेशो गुडाकेशेन भारत। सेनयोरुभयोर्मध्ये स्थापयित्वा रथोत्तमम्।।1.24।।
भीष्मद्रोणप्रमुखतः सर्वेषां च महीक्षिताम्। उवाच पार्थ पश्यैतान्समवेतान्कुरूनिति।।1.25।।
तत्रापश्यत्स्थितान्पार्थः पितृ़नथ पितामहान्। आचार्यान्मातुलान्भ्रातृ़न्पुत्रान्पौत्रान्सखींस्तथा।।1.26।।
श्वशुरान्सुहृदश्चैव सेनयोरुभयोरपि। तान्समीक्ष्य स कौन्तेयः सर्वान्बन्धूनवस्थितान्।।1.27।।
अर्जुन उवाच कृपया परयाऽऽविष्टो विषीदन्निदमब्रवीत्। दृष्ट्वेमं स्वजनं कृष्ण युयुत्सुं समुपस्थितम्।।1.28।।
सीदन्ति मम गात्राणि मुखं च परिशुष्यति। वेपथुश्च शरीरे मे रोमहर्षश्च जायते।।1.29।।
गाण्डीवं स्रंसते हस्तात्त्वक्चैव परिदह्यते। न च शक्नोम्यवस्थातुं भ्रमतीव च मे मनः।।1.30।।
निमित्तानि च पश्यामि विपरीतानि केशव। न च श्रेयोऽनुपश्यामि हत्वा स्वजनमाहवे।।1.31।।
न काङ्क्षे विजयं कृष्ण न च राज्यं सुखानि च। किं नो राज्येन गोविन्द किं भोगैर्जीवितेन वा।।1.32।।
येषामर्थे काङ्क्षितं नो राज्यं भोगाः सुखानि च। त इमेऽवस्थिता युद्धे प्राणांस्त्यक्त्वा धनानि च।।1.33।।
आचार्याः पितरः पुत्रास्तथैव च पितामहाः। मातुलाः श्चशुराः पौत्राः श्यालाः सम्बन्धिनस्तथा।।1.34।।
एतान्न हन्तुमिच्छामि घ्नतोऽपि मधुसूदन। अपि त्रैलोक्यराज्यस्य हेतोः किं नु महीकृते।।1.35।।
निहत्य धार्तराष्ट्रान्नः का प्रीतिः स्याज्जनार्दन। पापमेवाश्रयेदस्मान्हत्वैतानाततायिनः।।1.36।।
तस्मान्नार्हा वयं हन्तुं धार्तराष्ट्रान्स्वबान्धवान्। स्वजनं हि कथं हत्वा सुखिनः स्याम माधव।।1.37।।
यद्यप्येते न पश्यन्ति लोभोपहतचेतसः। कुलक्षयकृतं दोषं मित्रद्रोहे च पातकम्।।1.38।।
कथं न ज्ञेयमस्माभिः पापादस्मान्निवर्तितुम्। कुलक्षयकृतं दोषं प्रपश्यद्भिर्जनार्दन।।1.39।।
कुलक्षये प्रणश्यन्ति कुलधर्माः सनातनाः। धर्मे नष्टे कुलं कृत्स्नमधर्मोऽभिभवत्युत।।1.40।।
अधर्माभिभवात्कृष्ण प्रदुष्यन्ति कुलस्त्रियः। स्त्रीषु दुष्टासु वार्ष्णेय जायते वर्णसङ्करः।।1.41।।
सङ्करो नरकायैव कुलघ्नानां कुलस्य च। पतन्ति पितरो ह्येषां लुप्तपिण्डोदकक्रियाः।।1.42।।
दोषैरेतैः कुलघ्नानां वर्णसङ्करकारकैः। उत्साद्यन्ते जातिधर्माः कुलधर्माश्च शाश्वताः।।1.43।।
उत्सन्नकुलधर्माणां मनुष्याणां जनार्दन। नरकेऽनियतं वासो भवतीत्यनुशुश्रुम।।1.44।।
अहो बत महत्पापं कर्तुं व्यवसिता वयम्। यद्राज्यसुखलोभेन हन्तुं स्वजनमुद्यताः।।1.45।।
यदि मामप्रतीकारमशस्त्रं शस्त्रपाणयः। धार्तराष्ट्रा रणे हन्युस्तन्मे क्षेमतरं भवेत्।।1.46।।
सञ्जय उवाच एवमुक्त्वाऽर्जुनः संख्ये रथोपस्थ उपाविशत्। विसृज्य सशरं चापं शोकसंविग्नमानसः।।1.47।।
modern: Dhritarashtra said: On the field of dharma, the field of Kuru, assembled and eager to fight, what did my sons and the Pandavas do, O Sanjaya? (1.1)
Sanjaya said: Having seen the Pandava army arrayed, King Duryodhana then approached his teacher and spoke these words. (1.2)
Behold, O teacher, this great army of the sons of Pandu, arrayed by your wise disciple, the son of Drupada. (1.3)
Here are heroes, mighty archers, equal to Bhima and Arjuna in battle: Yuyudhana, Virata, and the great warrior Drupada. (1.4)
Dhrishtaketu, Chekitana, and the valiant king of Kashi; Purujit, Kuntibhoja, and Shaibya, the foremost of men. (1.5)
The mighty Yudhamanyu, the valiant Uttamaujas, the son of Subhadra, and the sons of Draupadi—all great warriors. (1.6)
But know, O best of Brahmins, those who are distinguished among us—the leaders of my army. I name them for your information. (1.7)
Yourself, Bhishma, Karna, and the victorious Kripa; Ashvatthama, Vikarna, and the son of Somadatta. (1.8)
And many other heroes, ready to give up their lives for my sake, armed with various weapons, all skilled in war. (1.9)
This army of ours, protected by Bhishma, is insufficient; but that army of theirs, protected by Bhima, is sufficient. (1.10)
Therefore, stationed in your respective positions at all the strategic points, you must all protect Bhishma alone. (1.11)
To enliven his spirits, the aged grandsire of the Kurus, the glorious one, roared like a lion and blew his conch loudly. (1.12)
Then conches, drums, cymbals, tabors, and trumpets were sounded all at once, and the noise was tumultuous. (1.13)
Then, standing in a great chariot yoked with white horses, Madhava and the Pandava blew their divine conches. (1.14)
Hrishikesha blew the Panchajanya; Dhananjaya blew the Devadatta; and the wolf-bellied Bhima, of terrible deeds, blew the great conch Paundra. (1.15)
King Yudhishthira, son of Kunti, blew the Anantavijaya; Nakula and Sahadeva blew the Sughosha and Manipushpaka. (1.16)
The king of Kashi, the supreme archer; Shikhandi, the great warrior; Dhrishtadyumna, Virata, and the invincible Satyaki. (1.17)
Drupada, the sons of Draupadi, and the mighty-armed son of Subhadra—all blew their respective conches, O lord of the earth. (1.18)
That tumultuous sound, reverberating through the sky and the earth, tore the hearts of the Dhartarashtras. (1.19)
Then, seeing the Dhartarashtras arrayed, the Pandava, whose banner bore the emblem of Hanuman, took up his bow, ready for the clash of weapons. (1.20)
Arjuna said: O Hrishikesha, place my chariot between the two armies, O Achyuta. (1.21)
So that I may see those who stand here eager to fight, and with whom I must engage in this great battle. (1.22)
I wish to see those who have assembled here, ready to fight, seeking to please the evil-minded Dhartarashtra in battle. (1.23)
Sanjaya said: Thus addressed by Gudakesha, O Bharata, Hrishikesha placed the best of chariots between the two armies. (1.24)
In front of Bhishma, Drona, and all the kings, he said: O Partha, behold these Kurus assembled here. (1.25)
There, Partha saw stationed fathers, grandfathers, teachers, maternal uncles, brothers, sons, grandsons, and friends. (1.26)
He saw fathers-in-law and well-wishers in both armies. Seeing all these kinsmen arrayed, the son of Kunti (1.27)
Arjuna said: Overwhelmed with deep compassion, sorrowfully he spoke: O Krishna, seeing my own people eager to fight, (1.28)
My limbs fail, my mouth dries up, my body trembles, and my hair stands on end. (1.29)
The Gandiva bow slips from my hand, my skin burns, I cannot stand steady, and my mind seems to whirl. (1.30)
I see omens of evil, O Keshava. I foresee no good from killing my own people in battle. (1.31)
I desire not victory, O Krishna, nor kingdom, nor pleasures. What is kingdom to us, O Govinda? What are enjoyments or even life? (1.32)
Those for whose sake we desire kingdom, enjoyments, and pleasures—they stand here in battle, having given up their lives and wealth. (1.33)
Teachers, fathers, sons, and also grandfathers; maternal uncles, fathers-in-law, grandsons, brothers-in-law, and other relatives. (1.34)
I do not wish to kill them, even if they kill me, O Madhusudana. Even for the sake of the kingdom of the three worlds, how much less for the earth? (1.35)
What joy would we have, O Janardana, by killing the Dhartarashtras? Sin would only cling to us if we kill these aggressors. (1.36)
Therefore, we should not kill the Dhartarashtras, our own kinsmen. How can we be happy, O Madhava, by killing our own people? (1.37)
Even if these, with minds overwhelmed by greed, see no evil in destroying the family or sin in hostility to friends, (1.38)
Why should we not learn to turn away from such sin, O Janardana, we who see the evil of destroying a family? (1.39)
When a family is destroyed, its eternal family traditions perish. When traditions are lost, the entire family is overtaken by adharma. (1.40)
When adharma prevails, O Krishna, the women of the family become corrupt. When women are corrupted, O Varshneya, intermixture of castes arises. (1.41)
This intermixture leads the destroyers of the family and the family itself to hell. Their ancestors fall, deprived of offerings of rice and water. (1.42)
By these sins of the destroyers of families, causing intermixture of castes, the eternal caste duties and family duties are destroyed. (1.43)
We have heard, O Janardana, that men whose family duties are destroyed inevitably dwell in hell. (1.44)
Alas! We are resolved to commit a great sin, for we are ready to kill our own people out of greed for the pleasures of the kingdom. (1.45)
If the Dhartarashtras, with weapons in hand, kill me in battle, unresisting and unarmed, that would be better for me. (1.46)
Sanjaya said: Having spoken thus on the battlefield, Arjuna sat down on the chariot seat, casting aside his bow and arrows, his mind overwhelmed with grief. (1.47)
